figures.caption: Figure 2: Cross-metric faithfulness-transfer deltas on OpenbookQA (top) and LogiQA (bottom), split by base model. The x-axis shows evaluation metrics, while colors indicate optimization targets. Points denote deltas from the corresponding base model; filled and hollow markers indicate SFT and DPO. Translucent violins show the spread of deltas for each target–evaluation pair, and downward triangles mark matched target–evaluation settings.
Figure 4: Parametric vs. contextual comparison across base models. Each row corresponds to one model.

Figure 1: Overview of our optimization-based meta-evaluation framework. We sample CoTs from a base model, score them using contextual or parametric faithfulness metrics, construct preference pairs, and optimize the model towards selected faithfulness objectives. We then evaluate cross-metric transfer across both faithfulness dimensions.
Table 16: Faithfulness scores of base, unmerged, and merged models on OpenbookQA with Qwen2.5-7B. Bolded values denote the best-performing configuration for a given faithfulness metric.

Table 13: Representative cases with large faithfulness-score improvements after DPO. Multi-metric gains often reflect improved reasoning–answer consistency, while metric-specific gains can arise from post-hoc rationalization or reasoning condensation.
Figure 5: Similarity analysis of task vectors of Llama3.1-8B on OpenbookQA.
Table 15: Faithfulness scores of base, unmerged, and merged models on OpenbookQA with Gemma3-4B. Bolded values denote the best-performing configuration for a given faithfulness metric.

Table 14: Representative parametric top-shift cases. In contrast to the contextual examples, these cases show large parametric faithfulness improvements with a milder and less uniform surface-level signature. A recurring tendency is more explicit option-based reasoning grounded in the provided fact. Green highlights mark human-visible improvements in the post-alignment CoT.

Table 12: Faithfulness gains on OpenBookQA and LogiQA by optimization target and evaluation metric. Rows denote the faithfulness metric used for optimization. Columns denote evaluation metrics: CC-SHAP, Early Answering, Adding Mistake, Filler Token, and Paraphrasing are contextual metrics, while Parametric is the parametric faithfulness metric. Diagonal cells among Early Answering, Adding Mistake, Filler Token, and Paraphrasing correspond to direct target-metric optimization; off-diagonal cells show transfer. CC-SHAP is contextual but evaluation-only (Feval) in our setup. The Cont. Avg. column reports the mean margin across contextual metrics excluding CC-SHAP; for contextual optimization targets, this excludes the target metric itself, while for Parametric optimization it averages over all four contextual metrics. Each cell reports the number of positive cases and the mean margin over the available base model–training pairs from Tables 10 and 11.
Figure 3: Parametric vs. contextual comparison across base models. Each row corresponds to one model.
